context: his Word is like a plumb line
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Let me remind you of what the Old Testament says. Turn to the book of Amos, if you will. Hosea, Joel, Amos, and go over, if you will, to the book of Amos, chapter number eight.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Behold, the day has come, saith the Lord God, that I will send a famine in the land, not a famine of bread, nor a thirst of water, but of hearing the words of the Lord.
point_summary: A famine of God's word is a divine judgment, invoked here as a warning against the spiritual dullness rebuked in Hebrews 5, where neglect of Scripture leads to stunted growth.

context: We're going to begin, however, in the book of Amos and Old Testament prophet, Hosea Joel Amos, and try to help you out there, Amos chapter 3.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I've titled the message this evening there from Amos 3. I used the entire verse as our title this evening. Verse number 3, can two walk together except they be agreed?
point_summary: Genuine unity is impossible apart from agreement with God and His Word, making Amos 3:3's question the governing principle for evaluating ecumenical affiliation and separation.

context: In Amos chapter number four, God goes through a series of dialogue, and in quick summary review He says there was famine, there was drought, there was blight, there was pestilence, and there was destruction. Yet for all of this have ye not returned unto Me, saith the Lord.
point_summary: In Amos 4, God sends successive judgments—famine, drought, blight, pestilence, destruction—each met with continued impenitence, culminating in the solemn warning "prepare to meet thy God."

context: And then you come down to verse number 12 of Amos four, and it says, therefore, in the very end of the verses, prepare to meet thy God, O Israel.
point_summary: After repeated judgments failed to produce repentance, Amos 4:12 issues the solemn divine ultimatum—"prepare to meet thy God"—signaling that unrepentant nations face direct, fearful encounter with God himself.

context: The words of Amos
point_summary: Amos 1:1 is used to establish the historical setting of Amos's prophecy—the reigns of Uzziah and Jeroboam—noting that Uzziah's pride and priestly presumption led to his leprosy.

context: who was among the herdmen of Tekoa, which he saw concerning Israel in the days of Uzziah, king of Judah.
point_summary: Amos 1:1 establishes the historical setting of Amos's prophecy—the reigns of Uzziah and Jeroboam—situating his words against both a morally compromised Judean king and a wholly wicked Israelite king.

context: the Lord will roar from Zion, and utter His voice from Jerusalem, and the habitations of the shepherds shall mourn, and the top of Carmel shall wither.
point_summary: God's roaring voice from Zion in Amos 1:2 establishes that the judgments pronounced throughout the following oracles carry divine authority, each introduced by the repeated phrase "Thus saith the Lord."

context: Verse number three, Amos 1. Amos 1 and verse number three. And so God is speaking, this is the Lord God Almighty, Amos is a prophet of God, and the Bible says, thus saith the Lord.
point_summary: The repeated phrase "Thus saith the Lord" in Amos 1:3 establishes that God Himself is the speaker of coming judgment, demanding that hearers give heed to His Word.

context: thus saith the Lord, for three transgressions of Judah and for four, I will not turn away the punishment thereof
point_summary: Judah's inclusion among the nations facing divine judgment establishes that God's people are not exempt from punishment when they despise His law and abandon His commandments.

context: because they have despised the law of the Lord and have not kept his commandments. And their lies caused them to err after the which their fathers have walked. But I will send a fire upon Judah and it shall devour the palaces of Jerusalem.
point_summary: Judah's inclusion among the nations facing divine judgment — specifically for despising God's law and failing to keep his commandments — demonstrates that proximity to God's covenant brings no exemption from his righteous punishment.

context: The next group in verses six, really throughout the end of the book of Amos, but contextually as we're looking at the immediate context of the pronouncement of judgment in verses six through eight of chapter two, Israel is in sight.
point_summary: Israel's inclusion among the nations facing divine judgment—after Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and Judah—demonstrates that God's people are not exempt from punishment when sin brings reproach.

context: hear this word, is how it starts in verse number one
point_summary: God's repeated judgments on Israel — famine, drought, blight, and pestilence — each concluded with the indictment that the people still had not returned to Him, illustrating the hardness of unrepentant sin under divine discipline.

context: look at the last part of the verse. It says, for this likeeth you, O you children of Israel, saith the Lord God
point_summary: Amos 4:5's closing phrase — "this liketh you, O children of Israel" — introduces a pattern of divine judgments (famine, drought, blight, pestilence) that Israel repeatedly refused to heed, each punctuated by "yet have ye not returned unto me."

context: and then it says in verse number six, and I also have given you cleanness of teeth in all your cities and want of bread in all your places, yet have ye not returned unto me, saith the Lord
point_summary: God's repeated judgments on Israel through famine, drought, blight, and pestilence each went unheeded, illustrating that divine chastisement does not automatically produce repentance.

context: yet have ye not returned unto me, saith the Lord
point_summary: Drought sent as divine discipline failed to produce repentance in Israel, illustrating the pattern Amos repeats five times: escalating judgments that nonetheless leave the people unturned toward God.

context: yet have ye not returned unto me, saith the Lord
point_summary: Crop blight in Amos 4:9 is one in a series of divine judgments—famine, drought, blight, pestilence, overthrow—each met with Israel's unrepentant refusal to return to the Lord.

context: yet have ye not returned unto me, saith the Lord
point_summary: Amos 4:10's pestilence is one in a series of divine judgments God sent upon Israel, each met with the same refrain of unrepentance: "yet have ye not returned unto me, saith the Lord."

context: I have overthrown some of you as God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah
point_summary: Even the catastrophic judgment of Sodom-and-Gomorrah-scale overthrow, cited as the climax of Amos's escalating divine chastisements, failed to move Israel to repentance—illustrating that sin hardens a nation against returning to God.

context: each of those people groups that we referenced in Amos 1
point_summary: The nations catalogued in Amos 1–2:8 serve as historical evidence that God executes righteous judgment on wicked peoples before the final return of Christ, making present-day America's moral condition a live occasion for that same judgment.

context: in the first eight verses of Amos 2
point_summary: The nations catalogued in Amos 1–2:1–8 each received divine judgment in history before any final eschatological reckoning, illustrating that God executes righteous judgment on sinful peoples prior to Christ's return.

context: Shall not the day of the Lord be darkness and not
point_summary: Outward religious performance without a surrendered heart renders worship unacceptable to God, as Israel's offerings, feasts, and songs were rejected because their hearts were not right with Him.

context: I hate, I despise your feast days, and I will not smell in your solemn assemblies.
point_summary: God's rejection of Israel's feast days and sacrificial offerings in Amos 5:21 is cited as Old Testament evidence that the sacrificial system was inadequate apart from genuine obedience and right worship.

context: Though ye offer me burnt offerings and your meat offerings, I will not accept them, neither will I regard the peace offerings of your fat beast.
point_summary: Amos 5:22, read within its surrounding context, illustrates that God rejected Israel's sacrifices and offerings because the worship was hollow, underscoring the Old Testament sacrificial system's inadequacy apart from genuine obedience.

context: Take thou away from me the noise of thy songs, for I will not hear the melody of thy vials.
point_summary: God's rejection of Israel's worship — sacrifices, feasts, and songs — is cited alongside Jeremiah 6 to establish that the Old Testament sacrificial system was inadequate apart from genuine obedience.

context: But let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a mighty stream.
point_summary: God's rejection of Israel's ritual worship — sacrifices, feasts, and songs — because of concurrent idolatry illustrates the Old Testament sacrificial system's inadequacy apart from true devotion.

context: Have you offered unto me sacrifices and offerings in the wilderness forty years, O house of Israel?
point_summary: Israel's wilderness sacrifices are cited as evidence that the Old Testament sacrificial system was ultimately inadequate, with idolatry compounding the failure of mere ritual observance.

context: But ye have borne the tabernacle of your Molech and Chion, your images, the star of your God, which you made to yourselves.
point_summary: Amos 5:26 is cited as evidence of Israel's persistent idolatry, illustrating the broader Old Testament pattern of corrupt worship that rendered the sacrificial system inadequate before God.

context: Therefore will I cause you to go into captivity beyond Damascus, saith the Lord, whose name is the God of hosts.
point_summary: Amos 5:27's pronouncement of captivity beyond Damascus is cited as evidence of Israel's idolatry and the inadequacy of their sacrificial worship, which God rejected because it was corrupted by false gods.

context: I want you to turn your Bibles, if you will, back to the book of Amos in chapter number eight
point_summary: Amos 8:11 is invoked to distinguish a spiritual famine—the withdrawal of God's word—from physical famine, warning that neglecting Scripture and preaching risks exactly this divine judgment.

context: In fact, the Bible makes it very clear in verse number 11 of Amos chapter eight. The Bible says, behold, the days come, saith the Lord God, that I will send a famine in the land. Not a famine of bread nor thirst for water, but of hearing the words of the Lord.
point_summary: A famine of hearing the words of the Lord—not physical deprivation—is God's judgment on Israel, foreshadowing the 400 silent years when divine revelation ceased and the people perished without prophetic word.

context: Remember as I mentioned in the book of Amos in the fourth chapter after famine and drought and blight and pestilence and destruction that God says, yet have ye not returned unto me, saith the Lord.
point_summary: Amos 4 is cited as a catalogue of divine chastisements—famine, drought, blight, pestilence, and destruction—after each of which God declares that Israel still did not return to Him.

context: Amos 8 and verse number 11, it's 11 and 12, Amos says this under the inspiration of God
point_summary: A coming famine not of bread but of God's word is cited as prophetic backdrop to the sermon's claim that we are living in an age of deception where hearing the Lord's words grows scarce.

context: Behold, the days come, saith the Lord God, that I will send a famine in the land, not a famine of bread, nor a thirst for water, but of hearing the words of the Lord.
point_summary: Amos 8:11 is read as prophetic warning that a coming famine of hearing God's Word—not physical bread—parallels the New Testament picture of people turning from sound doctrine in the last days.

context: And we find that no doubt as we would consider the warnings or the statements that are given and you couple those two truths together from Amos 8 and from 2 Timothy 4
point_summary: When Amos 8 is paired with 2 Timothy 4, the combined warning is that rejecting truth leads to disaster and leaves a society vulnerable to widespread deception.

context: how can two walk together except they be agreed
point_summary: Amos 3:3 is invoked to ground the principle that those serving together in the church must share doctrinal agreement, not merely skill or availability.

context: can two walk together except they be agreed
point_summary: Amos 3:3 is cited as the Old Testament foundation for the sermon's central theme: that genuine fellowship and cooperation require agreement, establishing the biblical basis for separation.
